sentences.text: And we were driven on to the airport, and we had to unload a lot of railway trucks of cement.
Each bag of the cement weighs 50 kilos, and there were quite a number of elderly people.
And it wasn't so easy for them to schlep 50 kilos, and the Germans stood behind and pushed us to run quickly with the bags.
That night, we lost about 250 people.
What had happened, they used to go with the bag of cement and drop.
And once they dropped it, the officer in charge would come up, pull out his gun, and shoot him in the head and put his foot down.
And that was the end.
We were then kept right through the day-- right through the night and right through the day.
We didn't believe it, that we were going to be driven back to the ghettos.
We had in our mind, from there, they'll take us to the-- to the-- whatever, and they will-- they will shoot us.
But they brought us back to the airport-- to the ghetto, and we continued work again, the same, the daily work again, from different-- we lost a lot of people that night.
One day, before the-- we-- the last days of the war in Lithuania, the German communication-- post-communication moved in into the building-- into the house where we were.
And we were in one room, and the other room were the German communications.
They didn't ask any questions.
They just moved in, and that's it.
[INAUDIBLE] houses in our little coal [INAUDIBLE], and they're burning.
And we're afraid that our house will be-- will be hit in the next-- we don't know when-- and you-- and the house will catch fire.
And you will-- you will die in that hole.
So I started to cry, and I just started to cry, to beg her, Castula.
Castula was her name.
Castula, Castula, let us out.
And she says, but the Germans are here next door.
And we used to hear the Germans underground, and they used to walk around and talk between themselves.
And we used to hear them.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Germany had lost the war and so on.
They used to speak between themselves.
So I said, lock the door between.
Please, lock the door and let us out.
So she did so, and I jumped out from the hole.
And I then slipped out Ida and we couldn't stay on our feet.
We didn't-- we didn't stand on our feet for 100 days, so our feet were just-- anyway, but from-- from-- from fright, you've got to.
And she says, come.
I'll show you where there is a bunker from our people digged out for the protection of our people in case there is any trouble in this area, and my son-in-law will wait for you at the bunker.
And she takes us through that yard, and the yard is full of artillery and machine guns from the Germans.
But what we did is through a mattress.
A coil mattress from the farmer's bed went to Ida's head.
She looked real Jewish, you know, pretending that we were moving out the furniture from the house.
And she walked in front.
And I direct her where to walk, and I held behind, like this, the mattress.
Then we walked through the yard, and the farmer's wife walks in front of us.
And she showed us.
Go to this and this street.
See, Mr.
Pogir, that and that tree.
Go there, and there is my son waiting for you.
And there is a bunker.
And you will go down, and you will stay there.
So we go out.
The sun is so strong.
It was 3 o'clock in the afternoon, strong, and I couldn't see anything from not seeing so much and walking.
Anyway, we couldn't find so quickly the place.
So I went-- I sat Ida near a tree, and I went back to the farmhouse.
And I asked Castula to show me exactly where I walk through the Germans like this, between their machine guns and so on.
And, uh, then she came out again, and she pointed out that and that tree.
Go there, and go straight.
You see that tree?
All right, and this time we find it.
We find it, and we went down that bunker.
And the people inside, there were quite a number of people there already from the surroundings.
And they start to ask, who are these people, who are these people, to his son-in-law.
And he said, they were running.
And they came up to me, asked me whether I'll let them in here, so I let them in.
He didn't know-- I don't know who they are.
And then the next morning, 5 o'clock in the morning, the next morning, the fighting was finished.
We couldn't hear a shot.
So they asked me, that son-in-law asked me, as you speak Russian, you may sometimes see a Russian here.
Just stick out your head and see.
As I sticked out my head, I saw a tank standing about a couple of hundred meters away from us, a Russian tank with that [INAUDIBLE] Molot.
And I then went out, and I lift my hands up, high up.
And I walked towards them, shouting that I am Jewish, [RUSSIAN] in Russian.
[SPEAKING RUSSIAN] and walked, and then they called me up.
And I told them that there is a bunker here from a lot of Lithuanian people.
Come.
He-- he had the officer there from the Russians walked with me towards that bunker, and he then ordered all the people from the bunker to get out because they free.
And some of the people who saw me, some of them knew me.
They saw me.
They fell to my feet and kissed my feet and Ida's.
That was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the 8 of August, 1944.
I was then about 27.
From then onwards, we stayed another two days at the farm, and we made sure there is no fighting in this area.
And we walked towards Kaunas.
We reached Kaunas.
It was, oh, about 10 kilometers south from there, so we reached Kaunas.
We went to our flat, and we found our flat empty, without anything.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] We find our flat.
We went into the flat, and then the caretaker lent us a little mattress.
And we had to sleep on something, and we got some food and so on.
We start living again.
Fantastic, we couldn't get over it.
Me and Ida, we held-- we hugged each other, crying loud that we're free, Ida.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] And we free, Ida, and we free.
And we free, and we're living.
And we're living, and so on and so on.
It was fantastic feeling.
There-- there wasn't-- there wasn't any neighbors.
There was only a Lithuanian, one Catholic, a Lithuanian Catholic.
That was the-- the building was empty.
It was-- it was-- Jewish-- a Jewish building.
Jewish people lived in there.
But during the war, I don't know why that building was empty.
Anyway, we start-- and 10 days later, I got a job with a Russian unit, a military unit, and because of my language, Russian, and writing.
And I was-- they appointed me to be the manager of stores, big stores.
They had some stores, goods, which they schlepped from Germany out.
And it wasn't so much when I started, but then they filled up as time-- they filled it-- they schlep it out from Germany, all the goods.
And we lived first class.
We started to live a normal life.
Me being the manager of the stores, we had everything.
We had food, plenty, and so on.
We used to-- when we used to eat meat, we used to lock the door that nobody can sometimes go in and see that we eating meat and so on.
That's why everything was a luxury.
And then it started to come in groups.
Like, for example, it came in, 50 cases of, pane glass, glass.
And when Kovno was bombed, the glass was like gold.
So I reported this to the officer in charge, that 50 cases of glass came in.
And he just-- he says to me, it's 45.
So I said to him, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN] It's pretty set.
It's 50.
He says, don't be an idiot, in Russian.
Don't be a-- don't be a [RUSSIAN], an idiot.
Five cases fell off the truck, and I'll give you tomorrow.
And you go and sell them tomorrow on the black market, and I'll give you tomorrow a note.
So tomorrow, yes, I go out, and I knew Kovno well because I was working there before.
And I sold them, and I bring 25,000 rubles for the glass.
And I put them on his desk, and he counted half the money and then pushes up to me.
That's for you.
So I pushed myself-- I pulled myself back.
I didn't believe it.
I was never a gonif in my life, and all of a sudden, he forced me with that.
And I argued with him until he started to shout at me.
Well, take the money, or get the hell out of it all together.
So I took the money, I walked out, and that was the start of it.
Being in the stores, we used to have sometimes 30 German prisoners who were working for me because there used to come in a lot of goods.
And we had to lay them on the shelves, and I used to ask in the office to give me some labor.
And they used to send some.
And I used to get goods and then sell them stuff, sometimes unused suits and this and that and everything-- everything from Germany-- carpets, furniture, everything.
I wasn't allowed to be rough to them, I was told, but occasionally I used to give a smack one, but occasionally.
Some of them are-- uh, I tell you.
The ordinary army from the Germans, they even didn't know what's happening.
Only the SS, but an ordinary soldier, he didn't even know what's happening.
And even if he knew, he wouldn't say.
But what could I do to them?
I couldn't do anything else.
Obviously, I hate them like poison, but in my heart, but I couldn't show that I-- with I'm hatred to them with hate.
And I started to have money unbelievable because of stealing goods from the stores and selling them and sharing with him.
And then we decided-- and I worked for them for 2 and 1/2 years.
I got two gold medals with Stalin's head for running the stores well, so I was prompt.
No.
No, they wouldn't because Lithuania-- Lithuania-- the whole of Europe, the soil is mixed up with Jewish blood.
It's absolutely mixed up with blood.
They slaughtered people, unbelievable, for no reason whatsoever, because you were Jewish and so on.
I used to come to our shtetl-- I used to come-- I lost a friend of mine.
I begged him.
Also he survived, and he married the farmer's daughter.
There was Polish people, and for surviving him, he married their daughter.
And he used to walk around in the shtetl with a rifle on his shoulders.
And I used to say to him, Shalom.
His name was Shalom.
Shalom, why are you walking around-- in Yiddish.
You don't understand Yiddish, beside.
Why are you walking around with a rifle on your shoulders?
And said, ah, I'm [NON-ENGLISH].
To hell with them, in other words.
And one day, he had a farm after 15 kilometers away from the shtetl, and on many occasions, I was there with my motorbike with him on the farm.
I used to ride 15 kilometers with the motorbike, nothing.
And I used to go there, stay the night, and come back.
And one night, he was attacked, him, on the farm.
He was attacked.
And he used to-- he didn't go-- he was attacked by Lithuanian partisans.
They were shot-- that house was shot at from bullets, and he remained alive.
But he didn't go anymore to that.
He used to sleep in the corn, in the field, but he'd never go in that house to sleep overnight, or the farmer's neighbor, or something like this.
Anyway, one day, he traveled back to his farm, and he was shot by the Lithuanian partisans.
And they killed him.
Me and a friend of mine which I mentioned to you, he was in the Russian Army.
When he came back, he started-- he was a good Zionist.
He says, let's go and look for Yiddish kids, maybe survive.
We'll find some Yiddish kids.
So we used to travel around all over the country to go Saturday, Sunday, when I didn't work.
He used to go on my motorbike and look for Yiddish kids.
And walking one day across a field, he was shot dead, also by partisans.
Did you find any children?
No, they wouldn't disclose.
They wouldn't disclose.
And we went to creche to look for them around, and me and Ida went to all the creches from Kaunas.
We used to go around, this way, that way.
We couldn't find any-- any of the Yiddish kids.
And we then decided, having so much money, that we [BACKGROUND NOISE] can't-- we saw the Yiddish people, Yiddish boys, escaping from Lithuania to Poland they go to Israel.
And we had made up our mind that we were going to Israel, me and Ida.
Ida was really start nagging me.
She was afraid to stay here because we've got so much money.
We built in the kitchen a double-decker for the money, so much money we had.
And eventually, they say in Jewish that [SPEAKING YIDDISH].
If they catch a gonif, they hang him.
And so we had in our mind, one day, they'll catch me.
They'll arrest me, and 10 years jail is nothing to them.
And that's the end of our life, so we decided we are going to escape.
And in December, '46, we made false documents.
We paid 3510 ruble gold.
It was a lot of money.
That was, like, $35,000.
We had the money.
So what?
We paid, and we got false documents.
We had false visas, and we escaped from Lithuania to Poland.
Where about in Poland did you go?
We go to Lodz.
First, we escaped first from Kovno to Vienna-- Wein-- then Baranowicze with a Jeep to Baranowicze.
And on the way to Baranowicze, we were stopped by a Russian officer to show our documents, whether we had documents.
The driver of the car had a document from the kibbutz commissar.
He had a document that he's allowed to travel.
So he takes out his document, and he shows like this, and the officer says, go.
And we lying in the back.
You know, it's mazel.
So we arrived at Baranowicze, two couples-- me and a friend of mine with two kids.
We have got Selwyn with us, our little boy, and Selwyn was born in 1945, by the way.
I didn't say that.
He was born in 1945.
So we've got Selwyn with us, a tiny, little boy, and a couple.
So I helped the couple-- the train arrived, and I helped the couple into the train.
And a woman conductor comes up to the door.
She puts out a foot in the door, and she said, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means that's full.
The rail-- the truck is full, and she didn't let us in.
We remained behind, and the railway-- and the whistle-- the railways went on.
And it was 4 o'clock in the afternoon.
December is cold.
In Lit-- it's already in Lithuania is winter.
And so we got the baby, and the baby start to nag us as [INAUDIBLE] and something like this.
And we walked from house to house, and nobody let us in.
Jew?
[NON-ENGLISH]?
Jew?
Jew?
And nobody let us in.
Eventually, one Russian woman let us in after about a kilometer away from the railways.
We walked from house to house.
Let us in, and she was quite friendly.
She made hot water for us, so we washed around Selwyn.
And we changed him and we-- the baby, and we had a cup of tea.
And about 7 o'clock in the evening, who walks into the house?
The officer in charge of the police walks into this house, but he was a nice man.
And we told him a story that a brother of mine is going through from Berlin to Moscow, and we coming to meet him here.
We want to see each other.
That means there is an extension which it calls Brest-Litovsk.
Anyway, he says, look here. 10 o'clock is the next-- 10 o'clock at night is the next train.
So he says to us, it is really dangerous for you to walk because we're getting reports that people are robbing people and killing people on this street where you- where you have to go.
But don't worry, he says.
I'll take you to the station-- him.
So he took out his gun from his back, he held it in his, and he walked in front of us.
And we walked behind him, and he took us to the station.
Wasn't that nice of him?
Anyway, instead of 10 o'clock at night, the train to arrive, it arrived 6:00 in the morning.
So we were lying at the under station the whole night, but this is-- and then we went to the next-- the next-- Brest-Litovsk was the next station.
And then we went to-- from Brest-Litovsk is already the border.
And we went through the border.
The border, they searched, unbelievable, and we had gold on us, plenty.
So especially Ida had in her boots-- she had a lot of-- we've still got two $25-- $20-- have you ever seen gold $20 American?
I've still got it in the bank, and 15 ruble-- 15 rubles-- a few 15 ruble Russian, gold.
We've still got it in the bank here, in Barclays Bank.
And-- And we met a girl, which I helped her out, and Ida helps her out while being in Kovno and me having such a good job.
And the girl was from Poland, a Yiddish meydl, and she didn't have any parents.
And where and what, she didn't know anybody [BACKGROUND NOISE] remained alive.
And we helped her out.
We used to ask her to come for meals and clothing and this.
And she had the-- and she says to Ida, Ida, they searching terrible.
They searching even inside a woman-- terrible, terrible, terrible.
And Ida comes to me.
She says, what are we going to do?
They searching terrible.
And I've got-- I'm so-- I'm treif.
You know what treif is?
I'm not kosher.
And what are we going to do?
So but I said, ask her if you can change the boots with her.
But she is already through the-- she's already through the customs, you see?
So they went into a toilet, the two of them, and they changed boots.
And that woman, the Polish meydl went away to Poland we didn't know where she was going, what she was going with all that money.
And Ida, was already-- she wasn't afraid anymore, so we went through the border.
We arrived in Lodz, and believe it or not, three weeks later, we were in Lodz.
That lady, that little medyl came to us with the money, with the boots, untouched.
She brought the boots.
Can you believe it?
So I go into the room, first.
We hid in the kibbutz, in [YIDDISH] kibbutz.
And I take out a $20-- I rip off here her sole, and I take out a $20 piece.
And I go up, and I give it to her.
And she wouldn't take it.
She said, you were so nice to us, to me.
In Kovno, you saved my life.
Why should I take money from you?
And she didn't took it, and we haven't seen her since.
And we went through, and we lived.
Six weeks we were in Lodz, in a kibbutz.
We then crossed to Czechoslovakia.
We were a couple of months in Bratislava.
The Zionist Federation.
And they directed.
We were already groups.
Like the border from Poland to Czechoslovakia, we moved in.
It was 11 o'clock in the morning, and then we then went on from Czechoslovakia to Austria.
In Austria, yeah, have you ever been to Austria, no?
Wein, Salzburg, Innsbruck, Seefeld, and Linz.
Have you been to Linz?
We've been then-- we came for Pesach.
For Pesach.
I mean, we escaped December, and this is Pesach.
That's about four months, isn't it?
We were in Linz.
Linz, the Zionist Federation in Linz find out that there is Jewish people, and they brought us matzo and chicken and egg product.
And it was fantastic, how they handled us.
And from there, we crossed into Salzburg, from Linz to Salzburg by train.
In Salzburg, we were four months in a camp, in an American Zone camp, and from there we went on near the border, which it was Seefeld.
And then the Zionist organization organized for kids-- Selwyn was then a little bit bigger-- for kids to go on holiday.
Selwyn was already 2 and 1/2.
All right?
We were about 150 people.
Yeah.
No, we didn't work.
We didn't work.
There was no work to be done.
We lived on-- on money given to us from the Zionist Federation or from where.
I don't know.
We didn't work.
I wasn't working, not even one day there.
And the main objective was to go to--
To go to Israel.
And then from Salzburg, the group went off.
The woman in charge of the kids, of her kids, every woman had 10 kids.
And she wouldn't take Selwyn because he was too small, so I gave her a $10 note.
And it was kosher, and she took him.
We were.
We were because when the train went off, we don't know where they go, when they go.
But they taking him-- they take him across the border, and one day, we will go.
So Ida fainted.
On the Salzburg station, she fainted.
There we lost a little kid, and here, we're letting go a little boy which we don't know whether we'll see him ever again and so on.
So she fainted on the Salzburg station.
Anyway, I revived Ida.
Ida, come.
So-- and I took her back.
And from there, we crossed the-- the Alps.
Ida went through first, and in 1947, in Israel, there were hung four British soldiers if you refer that sometimes.
You do?
So the British government instructed the countries around it not to let through any Jews, so we were stuck there.
We were stuck in a little dorf, Seefeld, called Seefeld.
But three weeks later, they wake up Ida, 3 o'clock in the morning.
Ida, come.
We are going to cross the border.
So they packed them into trucks with Red Cross outside, big Red Cross canvas, and then drive them up to the Austrian-Italian border, and they crossed the Alps by foot, the ladies.
They weren't caught, and we were held up.
The men were held up later.
And then a couple of weeks later, we were going.
We were also about 150, men only.
The ladies went already across, the mothers from the kids.
Not everybody had a kid, but most of them.
And we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were caught five times on the border between Austria and Italy.
My group was caught five times.
We nearly starved there from starvation.
They used to give us a little bit of soup and an orange a day and keep us for a couple of days and pack us into trucks and go back on no-man's land to go back to Austria.
